subject: English
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 35 minutes × 2 periods = 70 minutes
  Row 2: 40 minutes × 2 periods = 80 minutes
Total: 4 periods · 2h 30min

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Answer three comprehension questions about the poem

phases.description: Teacher reads the poem 'Colours' aloud expressively (pp. 4–5), then leads a choral reading: class reads together once slowly, then at a natural pace. Teacher points to each colour word as it is read.

Shared reading: Teacher and students read the poem together again, pausing after each stanza for students to restate in their own words what happened. Teacher draws attention to the contrast 'You have … I have …' and the resolution 'we have a full pack.'

Reading task — Let us think A (p. 5): Students answer the three comprehension questions (How many children? Why share? How do they get a full pack?) orally first in pairs, then write their answers in the textbook.
Pair review: Partners swap books and read each other's answers aloud. Teacher takes whole-class responses for question 3, reinforcing the poem's key message that sharing makes both children richer.

periods.teacher_notes: This is a 40-minute period; the extra five minutes allow unhurried writing after oral rehearsal. Guide students to write complete sentences rather than single words — model one answer on the board before independent writing. The poem's contrast device ('you / I / we') is worth highlighting as it mirrors the friendship theme from Section A.

periods.activity_title: Sharing Crayons — Paired Talk and Colour-Reading Game

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Read the colour of the ink, not the word

phases.description: Warm-up: Teacher holds up a crayon (or draws a coloured square) and asks 'What would you draw with only these three colours — green, blue, red?' Students respond quickly in pairs.

Guided conversation — Let us think B, prompts 1 and 2 (p. 5): 'Do you share anything with your friends?' and 'What would you draw with three crayons?' Pairs discuss for three minutes; four or five pairs share with the class. Teacher models a complete spoken sentence for hesitant students.

Colour-reading game — Let us speak B / Have fun! (p. 7): Teacher explains the rule — read the colour of the ink, not the printed word. Teacher models one example, then the class attempts the grid together, first slowly row by row, then faster as a choral drill.

Cool-down reflection: Teacher asks, 'Which colour word was hardest to say in the wrong way? Why?' Students share responses. Teacher previews that next period will focus on consonant-cluster words from the poem.

periods.teacher_notes: The colour-reading game (Stroop-type task) is cognitively demanding for Grade III; keep the first pass slow and choral before any individual attempts. Prompt 3 from Let us think B (drawing activity) is better suited as a free-time or art-integrated task and is left unanchored in this plan; flag it as optional classroom extension. No homework tonight.
materials: Crayons or coloured chalk for demonstration

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Say-aloud consonant-cluster words: gr, cr, dr, pr
Circle the action words from the list

homework.task_brief: Let us think (p.6): Rearrange letters to find hidden colour words

phases.description: Teacher writes 'green' and 'crayon' on the board and underlines 'gr' and 'cr'. Students say both words aloud three times; teacher explains that two consonants together at the start of a word make a cluster.

Phonics drill — Let us speak A (p. 6): Teacher leads choral reading of all four cluster columns (gr, cr, dr, pr), pointing to each word. Students then form one oral sentence per cluster word of their choice and share with a partner.

Action-word task — Let us learn A (p. 7): Teacher writes five words on the board (laugh, carrot, dance, leg, eat) and models identifying 'laugh' as an action word. Students then work individually to circle all action words in the full list in their textbooks.

Quick whole-class check: Teacher reads each word; students signal thumbs-up for action words. Disputed cases are discussed briefly. Teacher issues the homework: anagram/colour rearrangement task (p. 6).

periods.teacher_notes: Students may circle nouns that feel 'active' (e.g. 'sun', 'potato') — reinforce the definition: action words are things you DO, not things you see. The Let us learn B word-search grid (p. 8) is not anchored this period due to time; flag it as a self-study or early-finisher task. Homework: rearrange colour letters (p. 6, five items).

periods.activity_title: Alphabetical Order and Compound Word Friends

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Write five colours in alphabetical order
Match word-friends to form compound words

phases.description: Homework review: Teacher calls on three students to say their answers to the colour-anagram homework from Period 6. Class confirms or corrects together.

Alphabetical order task — Let us learn / alphabetical order (p. 13): Teacher recaps the alphabet sequence by leading a quick A–Z chant. Students then arrange YELLOW, BLUE, PURPLE, GREEN, RED alphabetically in their textbooks individually.

Whole-class review: Teacher elicits the correct order (BLUE, GREEN, PURPLE, RED, YELLOW) and writes it on the board. Students self-correct; teacher briefly explains how to compare second letters when first letters match.

Beyond-text task — Let us do (p. 14): Teacher reads the introductory sentence aloud — 'Like you, words also have friends.' Students match word-pairs (rain+bow done; rain+paper, blue+ball, basket+worm, earth+berry) and write the compound word. Teacher circulates and offers picture-clue prompts.

Closing celebration: Teacher displays the five compound words on the board. Students read them aloud together and teacher asks, 'Which compound word do you find the most surprising?' One or two responses end the chapter on a cheerful note.

periods.teacher_notes: The Let us learn C alphabet sentences (pp. 8–12) are extensive and are left unanchored due to time constraints; direct students to read them independently or during library time as a fun alphabet-recitation activity. Period 7 being 40 minutes allows both word_work and beyond_text to be completed without rushing. The chapter theme of friendship is reinforced by framing compound words as 'word-friends' in the beyond_text task.

items.source_lo: Student answers literal and inferential comprehension questions using a short poem as the vehicle.
items.source_context: Two-stanza poem about sharing crayons; colour words; sharing resolves scarcity.
items.item_stem: Read the poem 'Colours' and choose the correct answer.

options.label: A
options.text: The two children each have all the colours they need, so they do not share.

options.label: B
options.text: Neither child has a full pack of crayons alone, but sharing gives them all the colours they need.

options.label: C
options.text: One child has a full pack and gives some crayons to the other child.

options.label: D
options.text: The children buy a new pack of crayons at the shop.

what_each_option_reveals.A: Students who choose this have missed the poem's premise — both children lack colours, neither has a full set.
what_each_option_reveals.C: Suggests a misreading where one child is generous rather than both being incomplete; probe whether the student can distinguish 'giving from surplus' from 'sharing out of mutual need'.
what_each_option_reveals.D: Introduces a plot element not present in the poem; the student may be drawing on real-world experience rather than the text.

items.source_lo: Student participates in guided pair and whole-class conversation using a poem as the vehicle.
items.source_context: Sharing crayons; pair crayon-colour talk; Stroop colour-reading game.
items.item_stem: Work with a partner. In the poem 'Colours', one child needs red and the other needs black. Imagine you have only green, blue, and red crayons. Tell your partner: what would you draw with just these three colours? Listen to your partner's idea and say one thing you like about it.

expected_elements: Suggests a drawing using only green, blue, and red.
Listens and responds to partner's idea.
Uses at least one colour word from the poem.

items.source_lo: Student identifies and applies consonant clusters, action words, and alphabetical ordering using a poem as the vehicle.
items.source_context: Consonant clusters gr/cr/dr/pr; action-word identification; colours in alphabetical order.
items.item_stem:
The poem 'Colours' uses the word 'green' (consonant blend 'gr') and the word 'draw' (consonant blend 'dr'). Do the following tasks.
1. Fill in the blank with a word that begins with the consonant blend 'cr': The children use a _______ to colour their picture.
2. Three words are given below. Circle only the action words: share, crayon, draw, purple, swim.
3. Write the five colours from the poem in alphabetical order: RED, BLUE, GREEN, YELLOW, BLACK.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: 1. crayon (or any valid 'cr' blend word such as 'crab', 'crane' — accept any correct 'cr' word). 2. Action words: share, draw, swim. 3. Alphabetical order: BLACK, BLUE, GREEN, RED, YELLOW.

items.source_lo: Student forms compound words by combining word-pairs using a hands-on extension activity as the vehicle.
items.source_context: Compound words: rainbow, bluebell, newspaper, earthworm, strawberry formed from word-pairs.
items.item_stem: Just like friends help each other, some words join together to make a new word. Match each word in Column A with its partner in Column B to make a compound word. Write the new compound word in Column C.
items.visual_stimulus:
Column A | Column B | Compound Word
rain | worm | 
blue | paper | 
news | bow | 
earth | berry | 
straw | bell |

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: rain + bow = rainbow, blue + bell = bluebell, news + paper = newspaper, earth + worm = earthworm, straw + berry = strawberry
